label: ai
topic: Git version control tutorial for beginners
text:
If you have ever renamed a file "final_version_v2_ACTUALLY_FINAL.doc," you already understand the problem version control solves. You need a way to track changes over time, revert to earlier versions, and collaborate without overwriting each other's work. Git does all of this, and knowing the basics is essentially a prerequisite for working on any software team.

What Is Version Control?

Version control records changes to files over time so you can recall specific versions later. Think of it as an unlimited undo history for your entire project that also remembers who made each change and why.

Centralised systems like Subversion store history on a single server. If the server goes down, nobody can work. Distributed systems like Git give every developer a complete copy. You can work offline, commit locally, and synchronise when ready. This distributed nature is a key reason Git has become dominant.

Why Git Matters

Git was created in 2005 by Linus Torvalds for the Linux kernel. He designed it to be fast, distributed, and capable of handling very large projects. It is now used by virtually every major software company and open-source project.

Beyond professional development, Git is valuable for anyone working with text files — writers, researchers, data scientists. Anything that changes over time and benefits from reliable history is a candidate for version control.

Getting Started

Install Git from git-scm.com. Once installed, configure your identity with git config, setting your name and email globally so every project uses the same identity.

Repositories

A Git repository is a project folder that Git tracks. Create one by navigating to your project directory and running git init, which creates a hidden .git folder containing all tracking data. To join an existing project, use git clone followed by the repository URL, which downloads the entire project and its history.

The Basic Workflow

Git's daily workflow revolves around three concepts: the working directory, the staging area, and the repository.

The working directory is the files on your computer. When you edit a file, the change exists only here. Git knows it changed but has not recorded it.

The staging area is where you prepare changes for a commit. Use git add followed by the file name. This lets you selectively choose which changes to include, grouping related changes into logical commits.

A commit is a permanent snapshot of your staged changes. Create one with git commit, providing a descriptive message. Good messages are invaluable. "Fix bug" is nearly useless. "Fix off-by-one error in pagination that skipped the last page" tells a story.

Use git status to see which files are modified, staged, or untracked. Use git log to view the commit history.

Branches

Branches are one of Git's most powerful features. A branch is an independent line of development. Create one with git branch followed by a name, and switch to it with git switch.

The typical workflow is to create a branch for each feature or bug fix, make changes there, and merge back into the main branch when complete. This keeps the main branch stable and allows multiple people to work simultaneously.

Merging combines changes from one branch into another. In simple cases, Git does this automatically. When two branches change the same lines of the same file, Git declares a merge conflict, which you resolve manually.

Moving Forward

This covers the fundamentals. Remote repositories on platforms like GitHub enable team collaboration. Features like rebasing and stashing provide finer control. But the core workflow — edit, stage, commit, branch, merge — will carry you through most daily work. Start here and expand as the need arises.
